title: Why trembling after a trigger is actually a good sign
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
Your body is not broken. It's completing something. 

 You're sitting on your couch, maybe scrolling your phone, and suddenly your leg starts shaking. Or...
htmlContent:
Your body is not broken. It's completing something.

You're sitting on your couch, maybe scrolling your phone, and suddenly your leg starts shaking. Or your hands tremble. Or your jaw quivers like you're cold, but you're not cold. Your first thought is probably panic. What's wrong with me? Am I having a seizure? Is this a panic attack? I used to think the same thing. I'd feel that tremor start in my chest or my thighs, and I'd try to clamp down on it, hard. I'd tense every muscle, hold my breath, and pray it would stop. I thought the shaking meant I was falling apart. I was wrong.

Here's the thing. Trembling after a trigger is not a sign that something is broken. It's a sign that something is finishing. It's your nervous system doing exactly what it was designed to do, completing a cycle that got interrupted years ago. Maybe decades ago. Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent his career watching animals in the wild shake off life-threatening experiences. A gazelle escapes a lion, and then it trembles. Not because it's weak. Because it's resetting. The trembling discharges the excess energy that built up during the threat response. Without that discharge, the energy stays trapped in your body. That's what trauma is, in a very real sense. Unfinished business.

Think about it. When you were a kid, or even last week, something happened that your nervous system read as a threat. Maybe it was a loud voice, a sudden memory, a feeling of being trapped. Your body went into fight, flight, or freeze. It mobilized massive amounts of energy to help you survive. But if you couldn't fight, couldn't run, and couldn't complete the escape, that energy had nowhere to go. So it stayed. It got locked in your muscles, your fascia, your nervous system. And now, years later, when a trigger reminds your body of that old threat, the energy starts to move again. The trembling is that energy finally being released. It's your body saying, "I'm safe enough now to let this go."

Why we stop the shaking (and why we shouldn't)

We've been taught to see trembling as weakness. As loss of control. As something to be ashamed of. I've had clients tell me they feel "crazy" when their body starts shaking after a flashback. They tighten up, they distract themselves, they reach for a drink or a screen. Anything to make it stop. I get it. I've done it too. The feeling is intense, uncomfortable, even scary. But here's what I've learned from watching my own body and the bodies of the people I work with. Stopping the shaking is like stopping a sneeze. You can do it, but the pressure builds somewhere else. It comes out sideways, as chronic pain, migraines, digestive issues, or that constant low-grade anxiety that never quite goes away.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about the ventral vagal pathway as our social engagement system. When we're in a state of safety, we can connect, rest, and digest. But when we're in a threat state, our dorsal vagal pathway can take over, leading to collapse or shutdown. Trembling is often a sign that you're moving from that dorsal shutdown state back into ventral safety. It's the bridge. And it's uncomfortable because the bridge is wobbly. You're leaving a familiar state of numbness or hyperarousal and entering a state you might not fully trust yet. That's okay. You don't have to trust it completely. You just have to let it happen.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is the Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). It's a simple device that creates resistance on the exhale, which activates the vagus nerve and signals safety to your brain. I've found it helpful when the trembling feels overwhelming and I need something to anchor my breath. It's not a cure, but it's a companion for the process.

The difference between shaking in terror and shaking in release

This is where it gets tricky, and I want to be honest with you. Not all trembling is the same. There's a difference between the shaking that comes from being in an active threat state and the trembling that comes from releasing a threat state. The first feels sharp, fast, and panicked. Your heart is racing, your breathing is shallow, and you feel like you need to escape. That's your sympathetic nervous system in overdrive. The second feels deeper, slower, maybe wave-like. It might start in your legs or your hips and move upward. You might feel heat or cold. You might yawn or sigh. That's your parasympathetic nervous system taking over. That's the release.

How do you tell the difference? You don't have to be a neuroscientist. You just have to notice what's happening in your body, without judgment. Stay with me here. When you feel the trembling start, pause. Don't try to stop it. Don't try to make it bigger. Just notice. Is your jaw clenched? Are your shoulders up by your ears? Are you holding your breath? If yes, you might still be in the threat response. If you can soften those areas, even a little, the trembling might shift from a panicked shake to a more rhythmic release. That's the good sign. That's the body saying, "I'm coming home."

I've worked with people who have been trembling for months after a traumatic event. They thought something was wrong. They went to doctors, got MRIs, tried medications. Nothing helped. But when they finally understood that the trembling was their body's way of processing, they stopped fighting it. They started lying down, putting their hands on their own bodies, and letting the shaking happen. Within weeks, the intensity decreased. The trembling became shorter, less frequent. Because they stopped treating it as the enemy and started treating it as a messenger. Wild, right? The thing they were most afraid of was the thing that was helping them heal.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link) by Peter Levine. It's the foundational text on how animals release trauma through the body and how humans can do the same. It helped me understand why my own body was trembling and why it was actually a sign of health, not sickness.

How to support the trembling, not suppress it

So what do you actually do when the trembling starts? First, you create conditions for safety. Your nervous system won't release if it doesn't feel safe enough. That might mean wrapping yourself in a blanket, sitting with your back against a wall, or putting your hand on your heart or your belly. It might mean reducing sensory input. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). They lower the volume of the world without blocking sound completely. I use them when I'm feeling overstimulated and need to let my body do its thing without the extra noise.

Second, you breathe. Not in a forced, "I must breathe perfectly" way. Just a slow, gentle exhale. That's the key. The exhale is the brake pedal for your nervous system. When you lengthen your exhale, you activate the vagus nerve and tell your body it's safe to rest. You can use the breathing tool I mentioned earlier, or you can just breathe out through your nose with a slight constriction in your throat, like you're fogging up a mirror. Do that for a few minutes and see what happens to the trembling. Often, it deepens. That's good. That means the release is happening.

Third, you don't narrate. This is hard for me because I'm a writer. I want to tell a story about the trembling. I want to figure out where it came from, what memory it's linked to, what it means. But the body doesn't need a story. It just needs to move. So if you can, resist the urge to analyze. Let the trembling be what it is. A physical event. A wave of energy. A completion. You can think about it later. Right now, your body is doing something incredibly intelligent. Don't interrupt it.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is the YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The pressure can help you feel grounded and contained, which makes it easier to let go of the tension that's ready to move. It's not a magic fix, but it's a tool that can help your body feel safe enough to tremble.

What if the trembling doesn't stop?

I want to address a fear that comes up a lot. What if I start trembling and I can't stop? What if it goes on for hours? What if I lose control completely? I've had those thoughts too. Here's what I've learned. The trembling will stop when the discharge is complete. Your body knows how much it needs to release. It's not going to keep going forever. Usually, a release cycle lasts a few minutes to maybe twenty minutes. If it goes longer, it might be that you're still in a threat state and the trembling is part of the activation, not the release. In that case, you might need to slow down, orient to your environment, and find something that feels safe. Look around the room. Name five things you can see. Feel the ground under your feet. Let your body know it's not in danger right now.

If you have a history of complex trauma, the trembling might come and go over weeks or months. That's normal. Your body has a lot to process. Janina Fisher, who wrote "Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors," talks about how trauma is stored in parts of the self. Different parts might need to release at different times. You might tremble about one thing today and something completely different next week. It's not linear. It's messy. And that's okay.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did you tense up when I mentioned complex trauma? Did you feel a wave of something? That's your system responding. That's the work happening right now, in this moment, as you read these words. You don't have to do anything special. Just notice. Let your body know you see it. That's often enough to start the next wave of release.

I'm not going to tell you that trembling is easy. It's not. It can be uncomfortable, exhausting, and emotionally raw. But I will tell you this. It's a sign that your nervous system is alive. It's fighting for you. It's trying to complete what was left unfinished. And that is a good sign. A really good sign. Not because the trauma is gone, but because your body is finally being heard.
